full_text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|2.56|> Just before the service, I heard Jen Courtney Keys say something that's really interesting to me.<|8.80|> <|9.92|> She's an elementary school teacher and she said that the Denver City School System has given them<|16.88|> <|16.88|> new rubrics or directions for how to teach math this year, especially in elementary school.<|25.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The goal is to teach math this year for elementary school students so that it invokes in the student wonder.<|7.96|> <|9.66|> Wonder, not frustration or fear.<|13.42|> <|13.42|> Jen happens to love math and she believes that is exactly how math should be taught.<|20.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Jen has an ally in Jesus today.<|5.76|> <|5.76|> This parable is about math and how, if looked at in the right light, it could provoke a<|14.26|> <|14.26|> lot of wonder about how heaven works, how God works, and how this world could work with<|21.96|> <|21.96|> a little grace too.<|24.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The math or the payroll really matters.<|5.10|> <|5.10|> This is a story that begins at sunrise.<|8.70|> <|8.70|> The owner of the vineyard is there.<|11.42|> <|11.42|> The first workers show up.<|13.18|> <|13.18|> They show up early.<|15.12|> <|15.12|> They show up early as the sun is rising and they stay all day until evening, which means<|20.66|> <|20.66|> they work about 12 hours.<|23.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> That's more than what we would consider a full day of work. That's overtime. That's a hard, hot, exhausting day.<|8.46|> <|9.44|> At 9 a.m., more workers show up. They, too, work from 9 until sundown.<|16.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Then at noon, more show up.<|2.24|> <|2.24|> They work from, you see where this is going, they work from noon until sundown.<|6.32|> <|6.32|> Then finally, 3 p.m., not finally, 3 p.m., a group shows up and the owner of the vineyard<|11.70|> <|11.70|> puts them to work and they work a few hours.<|14.62|> <|14.62|> And then finally, the stragglers, who knows who they are, who knows when they showed up<|19.76|> <|19.76|> when they did, but they show up and work about an hour.<|24.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> at the end of that long hot day.<|2.90|> <|2.90|> And as the sun is setting,<|4.84|> <|4.84|> the owner of the venue pays them one and all,<|8.12|> <|8.12|> and he pays them all the same thing.<|12.46|> <|12.46|> A daily wage.<|15.40|> <|15.40|> There's a scholar,<|17.18|> <|17.18|> and this is where the math really does matter.<|19.80|> <|19.80|> There's a scholar who says a daily wage is<|22.32|> <|22.32|> the equivalent of three to six days of food for a family.<|28.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.48|> Three to six days of food for a family.<|4.26|> <|4.38|> And the last were paid that just like the first.<|9.16|> <|10.92|> The ones who've worked all day, the ones who got there at sunrise,<|16.30|> <|17.36|> grumble and complain about how unfair this is.<|22.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Now, for decades, I've heard homilies preached on this very parable.<|5.96|> <|7.14|> And usually, the preacher moves pretty quickly, not in Episcopal circles.<|12.92|> <|13.10|> I've been in many other denominations.<|15.06|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Amen.<|1.94|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.52|> Usually the preacher moves pretty quickly to scolding those who got there first and grumble<|6.84|> <|6.84|> because they just don't realize how generous, what a good thing generosity is.<|13.38|> <|14.24|> But the reality is, and I really want to underline this,<|17.28|> <|17.82|> I suspect that most of us, I certainly can, relate to those who got there early.<|24.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.88|> The fact that those who came at the end<|2.42|> <|2.42|> and only worked one hour,<|3.56|> <|3.82|> that actually is unfair.<|6.72|> <|9.20|> You know, I notice,<|10.70|> <|12.30|> and some people on staff have probably heard,<|14.52|> <|14.62|> I know they've heard me say,<|15.68|> <|16.12|> oh, I've put in a lot of hours this week.<|18.20|> <|19.70|> You know, oh, I've been going above and beyond<|21.72|> <|21.72|> for the Lord.<|22.64|> <|27.28|> Amen.<|27.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> So they grumble about this, and the fact of the matter is noticing when we've gone above<|7.24|> <|7.24|> and beyond, noticing when someone in a business, a community, a family doesn't put in the<|16.54|> <|16.54|> time that we do, noticing that is logical.<|21.66|> <|21.66|> I suspect most, if not all of us, do notice.<|25.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The problem is not that these first workers grumble and complain.<|7.24|> <|7.24|> I actually don't think that's the problem.<|9.68|> <|9.68|> I think the problem is that they don't realize that the fact those who came last and get paid<|15.88|> <|15.88|> just as they do, the real problem is they don't notice that that's both unfair and fabulous.<|23.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It's fabulous.<|2.00|> <|2.00|> Why?<|3.00|> <|3.00|> Because we know what a wage, one day's wage, gets you.<|10.00|> <|10.00|> It gets you three to six days of food for your family.<|17.00|> <|17.00|> That is incredibly great news for that family.<|21.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And that leads, I think, to the other problem with these who work an entire day.<|5.28|> <|5.80|> The problem, again, is not that they complain.<|8.00|> <|8.46|> I suspect most of us would.<|10.10|> <|11.10|> The problem is they don't realize that it's fabulous, and they don't have the imagination<|15.68|> <|15.68|> to complain.<|16.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> to realize that in a week or two,<|3.40|> <|3.40|> or a few months or years,<|5.94|> <|5.94|> the roles could flip and switch.<|9.50|> <|9.50|> And if those who worked the hardest were unlucky<|12.44|> <|12.44|> and events went the wrong way,<|15.22|> <|15.22|> they too could show up at the end of the day<|18.18|> <|18.18|> and need every bit of that wage.<|21.48|> <|21.48|> And that wage for them would be unimaginably good news<|25.96|> <|25.96|> for them and for their family.<|28.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|2.00|> This parable, as several of them do, starts out by Jesus saying the kingdom of heaven is like this vineyard.<|10.88|> <|11.70|> It's not literally this vineyard, but it's like this vineyard.<|16.64|> <|16.80|> God, God's presence, God's ways are like this vineyard.<|22.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And so Jesus himself has already cued us before he tells us the parable that the owner of the vineyard is like God.<|9.50|> <|10.72|> And so the owner of the vineyard, after hearing the grumbling from the first who worked the whole day, says,<|16.28|> <|16.28|> Are you envious? Because I am so generous.<|20.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It's a poignant and pointed question.<|4.00|> <|4.00|> I think in all kinds of scenarios, you and I often suspect, especially as faithful people,<|11.60|> <|11.60|> we suspect that God is going to act like us, behave like us, treat other people the way<|21.08|> <|21.08|> we treat other people.<|23.16|> <|23.16|> I think we even sometimes think God votes the same way we do, right?<|28.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But the reality is so many of the stories and teachings of Jesus and of what he did overturns<|9.96|> <|9.96|> all of that.<|10.66|> <|11.92|> Our ways, our preferences should never be confused with God's.<|18.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> There's a great Mark Twain quote.<|5.62|> <|5.62|> Twain said that in the beginning God made man in his own image, and man being a gentleman<|14.66|> <|14.66|> has been returning the favor ever since.<|20.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.96|> This parable pulls in two directions.<|6.58|> <|7.92|> It pulls toward heaven, but it also pulls toward earth.<|12.00|> <|13.16|> I know the opening collect counsels us not to be anxious about earthly things, but to love things heavenlyly.<|20.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But the earthly things in this parable matter and matter immensely.<|4.68|> <|4.68|> Jesus is talking about wages.<|6.56|> <|6.56|> Jesus is talking about food.<|8.44|> <|8.44|> Jesus is talking about having all that they need to live and flourish.<|16.02|> <|16.02|> And so this parable should invite us not only to contemplate heaven and God's ways, but<|22.16|> <|22.16|> contemplate how wages and food work in our society.<|27.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We should always be thinking about what does a wage get another person?<|6.34|> <|7.10|> And that's why conversations and thoughts about what a living wage is are always urgent and even biblical.<|14.98|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Go.<|1.32|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We'll pray in a moment that God's will will be done on earth as it is in heaven.<|8.28|> <|8.28|> And I think that too is an invitation in this parable to imagine ways in which you and I<|16.22|> <|16.22|> can really be more generous, especially if we own businesses, if we're employers, if<|22.44|> <|22.44|> we oversee people.<|23.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> to pay close attention to how we can be generous and how what once one person receives in a<|6.86|> <|6.86|> wage, what it actually gets them.<|10.52|> <|10.52|> I remember once hearing a preacher give really pious and bad advice.<|22.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.44|> to a family that was struggling. The details don't matter so much, you can easily imagine them.<|9.12|> <|10.88|> And I shared that with my dad. And I'll never forget my dad said about that preacher,<|17.52|> <|20.16|> "He's so heavenly minded, he's no earthly good."<|26.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And I think the invitation in this parable is to be both.<|8.84|><|endoftext|>
